WALKERLAKE.EVENTS The LakeThe ArtistsVisit

Walker Lake & Bighorn Sheep Resilience in the Desert

July 1–31, 2026

The Bighorn Crossing

The Lake

Walker Lake has given up more than 150 feet of itself within a lifetime — its river drawn off upstream for corporate farming, until the fish went first, then the birds that came for them.

But not everything left. Nevada’s largest bighorn herd still comes down to the water, crossing the road it has always crossed — and the people of this small lake town stayed with it. Resilience is not resistance; it is staying, and finding beauty in what remains.

This July, around twenty artists — painters, printmakers, ceramicists, metalworkers, some local, some from across the country — gather at The Bighorn Crossing: a month of art to draw eyes to a dying lake, before it is gone. Free, all month, indoors and across the outdoor grounds — in connection with Artown, in partnership with the Walker Basin Conservancy, working to return water to the lake.

Curated by Micky Kim · The Bighorn Crossing

Spotlight

Virginia Dumas

Sculpture & poetry · Hawthorne, NV

Steel, bronze, clay, and stone — with verse written to stand beside them. Her Four Smiles, foundry-cast in silicon bronze, hangs in the show. She came to art through the foundry, casting and welding, and taught art, writing, and drama for years.

Patricia L. Cooke

Mixed-media fibers · Miami, FL

Hand-dyed, quilted color pushed toward beautiful excess — the natural world stitched until its abundance tips into the overwhelming. Cooke is Senior Lecturer of Sculpture at the University of Miami.

Joe Taylor

Sculpture · Red Lodge, MT

Colorful, large-scale stoneware that draws on the body and the natural world — “ambiguous and playful spaces” of curiosity and wonder. A long-term resident artist at the Red Lodge Clay Center, and the son of Walker Lake artist Jack Taylor.

Zachary Fields

Mokuhanga woodblock print · Pontotoc, MS

Non-traditional Mokuhanga — the Japanese tradition of water-based woodblock print — carved by hand, each material showing its own character, recognized by the International Mokuhanga Conference. Trained also in geology, he reads the land in layers and time.

Gary A. Funk

Pen & ink · Hawthorne, NV

Dot by dot, in stippled ink, from photographs he takes himself at Walker Lake — a loving series of bighorn drawn to preserve the area’s culture and history. He has lived in Babbitt and Hawthorne nearly his whole life.
